_meta.disclaimer: All medication information is provided for informational purposes only and does not constitute a prescription. Consult a licensed healthcare provider before starting, stopping, or changing any medication.

recommended_tests: Throat swab if bacterial infection is suspected
possible_medications: Decongestants (e.g. pseudoephedrine, oxymetazoline nasal spray)
Antihistamines (e.g. loratadine, cetirizine)
Analgesics / antipyretics (e.g. paracetamol, ibuprofen)
Cough suppressants (e.g. dextromethorphan)
home_care: Rest and increase fluid intake
Inhale steam or use a humidifier to ease congestion
Gargle warm salt water for sore throat relief
Honey and lemon in warm water for cough relief
warning_signs: Fever lasting more than 4 days
Difficulty breathing or chest pain
Symptoms worsening after 10 days instead of improving
common cold.follow_up: See a doctor if symptoms persist beyond 10 days or worsen at any point.

recommended_tests: PCR (RT-PCR) for influenza type and subtype
Chest X-ray if pneumonia is suspected
possible_medications: Antivirals (e.g. oseltamivir, zanamivir) if started within 48 hours of symptom onset
Analgesics / antipyretics (e.g. paracetamol, ibuprofen)
home_care: Strict bed rest
Stay well hydrated
Isolate from others for at least 24 hours after fever resolves
Use cool compresses and light clothing to manage high fever
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
Persistent chest pain or pressure
Severe or persistent vomiting
Confusion or sudden dizziness
influenza.follow_up: Review with a doctor if fever persists beyond 5 days or breathing difficulty develops.

possible_medications: Antibiotics (e.g. amoxicillin, azithromycin, doxycycline) for bacterial pneumonia
Antivirals (e.g. oseltamivir) if viral pneumonia is confirmed
Antipyretics (e.g. paracetamol)
Oxygen therapy for low blood oxygen levels
home_care: Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course without skipping doses
Rest completely and avoid physical exertion
Drink plenty of fluids to loosen secretions
Sleep propped upright to ease breathing
warning_signs: Worsening shortness of breath
Bluish lips or fingertips
High fever that does not respond to medication
Confusion or extreme drowsiness
pneumonia.follow_up: Seek immediate medical care if breathlessness worsens at any point during treatment.

recommended_tests: Allergy skin-prick testing or specific IgE
Chest X-ray to exclude other causes
possible_medications: Short-acting beta-2 agonists (e.g. salbutamol inhaler) for rapid relief
Inhaled corticosteroids (e.g. beclomethasone, fluticasone) for prevention
Long-acting beta-2 agonists (e.g. salmeterol) combined with inhaled corticosteroids
Leukotriene receptor antagonists (e.g. montelukast)
home_care: Identify and avoid personal triggers (dust, pollen, smoke, cold air)
Keep a symptom and peak flow diary
Always carry a reliever inhaler
Maintain a clean, dust-free home environment
warning_signs: Reliever inhaler not relieving symptoms
Difficulty speaking in full sentences due to breathlessness
Lips or fingertips turning blue
Severe chest tightness that does not improve
asthma.follow_up: Review inhaler technique and control with a doctor every 3-6 months, or sooner if symptoms worsen.

possible_medications: Antiplatelets (e.g. aspirin, clopidogrel)
Statins (e.g. atorvastatin, rosuvastatin)
Beta-blockers (e.g. metoprolol, carvedilol)
ACE inhibitors / ARBs (e.g. ramipril, losartan)
Nitrates (e.g. glyceryl trinitrate) for anginal chest pain
home_care: Adopt a heart-healthy diet low in saturated fat and sodium
Engage in regular moderate exercise as advised by a cardiologist
Stop smoking completely
Control blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar diligently
warning_signs: Sudden severe chest pain or pressure
Pain radiating to the arm, jaw, or back
Shortness of breath at rest
Sudden sweating, nausea, or fainting
heart disease.follow_up: Call emergency services immediately for sudden chest pain; otherwise attend regular cardiology review as scheduled.

recommended_tests: Blood pressure measurement on multiple separate days
possible_medications: ACE inhibitors (e.g. ramipril, lisinopril)
ARBs (e.g. losartan, valsartan)
Calcium channel blockers (e.g. amlodipine)
Thiazide-like diuretics (e.g. indapamide)
home_care: Monitor blood pressure at home and keep a log
Reduce dietary sodium intake
Follow a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy
Maintain a healthy weight through regular physical activity
Limit alcohol intake
warning_signs: Severe headache with blurred vision
Chest pain or shortness of breath
Sudden numbness or weakness on one side
Blood pressure reading above 180/120 mmHg
hypertension.follow_up: Review blood pressure control with a doctor every 1-3 months until stable, then every 6-12 months.

possible_medications: IV thrombolysis (e.g. alteplase) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset for ischaemic stroke
Antiplatelets (e.g. aspirin, clopidogrel) for secondary prevention
Anticoagulants (e.g. apixaban, warfarin) for atrial fibrillation-related stroke
Statins (e.g. atorvastatin) for secondary prevention
home_care: Attend all physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy sessions
Modify the home to prevent falls
Adhere strictly to all secondary prevention medications
Address modifiable risk factors such as blood pressure and smoking
warning_signs: Face drooping on one side
Arm weakness or numbness
Slurred or difficult speech
Sudden severe headache or vision loss
stroke.follow_up: Call emergency services immediately at the first sign of stroke symptoms (FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time).

possible_medications: Insulin (basal-bolus regimen) for Type 1 diabetes
Metformin as first-line therapy for Type 2 diabetes
SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g. empagliflozin, dapagliflozin)
GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g. semaglutide, liraglutide)
home_care: Monitor blood glucose regularly as directed
Follow a low-glycaemic-index, low-refined-sugar diet
Exercise regularly to improve insulin sensitivity
Inspect feet daily for cuts, blisters, or ulcers
Take medications consistently without skipping doses
warning_signs: Blood glucose persistently above target range
Signs of severe dehydration or confusion
Non-healing wounds or ulcers, especially on the feet
Fruity-smelling breath with rapid breathing (possible ketoacidosis)
diabetes.follow_up: Attend HbA1c review every 3-6 months and annual eye, kidney, and foot screening.

possible_medications: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4) as lifelong replacement therapy
Liothyronine (T3) occasionally added in select cases
home_care: Take levothyroxine at the same time every day on an empty stomach
Avoid taking calcium, iron, or antacids close to the medication dose
Eat a balanced diet supporting thyroid health
Engage in regular gentle exercise to manage fatigue and weight gain
warning_signs: Severe fatigue or difficulty staying awake
Significant unexplained weight gain
Slow heart rate with chest discomfort
Swelling of the face or extremities
hypothyroidism.follow_up: Check TSH 6-8 weeks after any dose change, then annually once stable.

recommended_tests: Stool microscopy, culture, and sensitivity if symptoms persist
Stool PCR panel for pathogen detection
Electrolytes and renal function to assess dehydration
possible_medications: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) as the cornerstone of treatment
Antiemetics (e.g. ondansetron) for persistent vomiting
Antibiotics only if a bacterial cause is confirmed
Zinc supplements, especially recommended for children
home_care: Drink ORS or clear fluids frequently in small sips
Resume eating bland, easily digestible foods as tolerated
Practice strict hand hygiene
Avoid dairy and fatty foods until fully recovered
warning_signs: Inability to keep any fluids down
Blood in stool or vomit
Signs of severe dehydration (dizziness, reduced urination)
Symptoms lasting longer than 7 days
gastroenteritis.follow_up: See a doctor if unable to tolerate fluids or if symptoms do not improve within 3-5 days.

recommended_tests: Upper GI Endoscopy if alarm symptoms are present
H. pylori testing (breath test or stool antigen)
possible_medications: Proton Pump Inhibitors (e.g. omeprazole, esomeprazole)
H2 receptor antagonists (e.g. famotidine)
Antacids (e.g. Gaviscon) for rapid symptomatic relief
Prokinetics (e.g. domperidone) if delayed gastric emptying contributes
home_care: Elevate the head of the bed by 15-20 cm
Eat smaller, more frequent meals
Avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime
Avoid trigger foods such as fatty, spicy, or caffeinated items
Maintain a healthy weight
warning_signs: Difficulty or pain when swallowing
Unintentional weight loss
Vomiting blood or black, tarry stools
Symptoms not improving with medication after several weeks
acid reflux.follow_up: See a gastroenterologist if symptoms persist despite 4-8 weeks of treatment.

recommended_tests: Clinical diagnosis based on headache history
MRI brain with contrast if atypical features are present
CT brain if sudden severe headache to exclude haemorrhage
possible_medications: Triptans (e.g. sumatriptan, rizatriptan) for acute attacks
NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, naproxen) for mild-moderate attacks
Antiemetics (e.g. metoclopramide) for associated nausea
Preventive options (e.g. propranolol, topiramate, amitriptyline) if attacks are frequent
home_care: Keep a headache diary to identify and avoid personal triggers
Maintain regular sleep, meal, and exercise schedules
Rest in a dark, quiet room at the onset of an attack
Apply cold packs to the forehead or neck
Stay well hydrated
warning_signs: Sudden 'thunderclap' headache unlike previous episodes
Headache with fever and neck stiffness
New weakness, numbness, or vision loss
Headache following a head injury
migraine.follow_up: See a neurologist if attacks occur more than 4 times per month or change in character.

recommended_tests: Thyroid function tests to exclude hypothyroidism
Full Blood Count to exclude anaemia
Vitamin B12, folate, and Vitamin D levels
possible_medications: SSRIs (e.g. fluoxetine, sertraline, escitalopram) as first-line antidepressants
SNRIs (e.g. venlafaxine, duloxetine) particularly if pain or anxiety co-exists
Mirtazapine if poor appetite or insomnia is prominent
home_care: Engage in regular aerobic exercise
Maintain social connections with trusted friends or family
Establish a consistent daily routine with regular sleep and meals
Access talking therapies such as CBT
Limit alcohol intake
warning_signs: Thoughts of self-harm or suicide
Inability to carry out daily activities
Complete loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities
Significant changes in appetite or sleep lasting more than 2 weeks
depression.follow_up: Regular follow-up every 2-4 weeks initially, then monthly once stable on treatment. Seek immediate help for any thoughts of self-harm.

recommended_tests: Thyroid function tests to exclude hyperthyroidism
Cardiac assessment (ECG) if palpitations are prominent
Blood glucose to exclude hypoglycaemia mimicking panic attacks
possible_medications: SSRIs (e.g. sertraline, escitalopram) as first-line treatment
SNRIs (e.g. venlafaxine) as an effective alternative
Buspirone as a non-sedating anxiolytic option
Beta-blockers (e.g. propranolol) for situational anxiety symptoms
home_care: Practice diaphragmatic breathing and progressive muscle relaxation
Engage in regular aerobic exercise
Limit caffeine, alcohol, and recreational drug use
Practice mindfulness meditation
Gradually face feared situations rather than avoiding them
warning_signs: Panic attacks with chest pain that could mimic a heart attack
Inability to leave the house or carry out daily tasks
Thoughts of self-harm
Severe physical symptoms such as fainting
anxiety disorder.follow_up: Review with a doctor or therapist every 2-4 weeks initially to assess treatment response.

recommended_tests: FBC and CRP if upper UTI is suspected
possible_medications: Trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin as first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI
Pivmecillinam as an alternative first-line option
Co-amoxiclav or cefalexin as second-line or in pregnancy
Urinary analgesics (e.g. phenazopyridine) for symptom relief
home_care: Drink plenty of fluids to flush bacteria from the urinary tract
Urinate promptly when the urge arises
Wipe front to back after using the toilet
Urinate after sexual intercourse
Avoid perfumed soaps or products around the genital area
warning_signs: Fever with chills or back pain (possible kidney infection)
Blood in the urine
Symptoms not improving after 48 hours of antibiotics
Nausea and vomiting accompanying urinary symptoms
urinary tract infection.follow_up: See a doctor if symptoms do not improve within 2-3 days of starting antibiotics.

recommended_tests: MRI or ultrasound of affected joints
possible_medications: Methotrexate as the anchor disease-modifying drug
Hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine, often combined with methotrexate
Biologic DMARDs (e.g. etanercept, adalimumab) for refractory disease
NSAIDs (e.g. naproxen) for symptomatic relief
Short-term corticosteroids during flares
home_care: Balance rest during flares with gentle exercise during remission
Apply heat for stiffness and cold packs for swollen joints
Use assistive devices to protect joints
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce joint stress
Quit smoking
warning_signs: Sudden severe joint swelling with fever
New chest pain or breathlessness
Numbness or weakness in hands or feet
Signs of infection at a joint (warmth, redness, fever)
rheumatoid arthritis.follow_up: Regular rheumatology review every 3-6 months to monitor disease activity and medication safety.

possible_medications: Standard first-line regimen: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol for 2 months, then Isoniazid and Rifampicin for 4 months
Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) co-prescribed with isoniazid to prevent neuropathy
home_care: Adhere strictly to the full treatment course, typically 6 months
Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing
Ensure good ventilation in living spaces
Eat a nutritious, high-calorie diet to support recovery
Avoid alcohol during treatment
warning_signs: Coughing up blood
Severe difficulty breathing
High fever not responding to treatment
Signs of liver toxicity such as yellowing of the skin or eyes
tuberculosis.follow_up: Attend monthly sputum monitoring and complete the full prescribed treatment course without interruption.

recommended_tests: Thick and thin blood film microscopy
possible_medications: Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy (e.g. artemether-lumefantrine) for uncomplicated malaria
IV Artesunate for severe or complicated malaria
Chloroquine for chloroquine-sensitive species
Primaquine for radical cure after checking G6PD status
home_care: Use insecticide-treated bed nets every night
Apply insect repellent on exposed skin at dusk and dawn
Eliminate stagnant water around the home
Complete the full antimalarial treatment course even if symptoms resolve early
warning_signs: Confusion or reduced consciousness
Difficulty breathing
Persistent vomiting unable to keep medication down
Dark or reduced urine output
malaria.follow_up: Seek immediate medical care for any high fever within 3 months of travel to a malaria-endemic area.

recommended_tests: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
possible_medications: IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav, metronidazole with cefuroxime) given pre- and post-operatively
IV analgesics (e.g. paracetamol, morphine) for pain relief
IV fluids for resuscitation and surgical preparation
home_care: Seek emergency care immediately if appendicitis is suspected
Do not take laxatives or apply heat to the abdomen
Do not eat or drink anything once appendicitis is suspected
Follow all post-operative wound care instructions
warning_signs: Sudden worsening of abdominal pain
Pain moving to the lower right abdomen
Fever with abdominal rigidity
Inability to pass gas or have a bowel movement
appendicitis.follow_up: This is a surgical emergency — seek immediate emergency department evaluation rather than routine follow-up.

recommended_tests: Serum tryptase if anaphylaxis is suspected
Oral food challenge for confirmed food allergy
possible_medications: Intramuscular adrenaline (epinephrine) as first-line treatment for anaphylaxis
Antihistamines (e.g. chlorphenamine, cetirizine) for mild-moderate reactions
Corticosteroids (e.g. hydrocortisone, prednisolone) to reduce risk of recurrence
Inhaled bronchodilators (e.g. salbutamol) for associated bronchospasm
home_care: Always carry two adrenaline auto-injectors if previously prescribed
Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying the allergy
Read food labels carefully and inform others of severe allergies
Avoid all known triggers and keep a written allergy action plan
warning_signs: Swelling of the throat, lips, or tongue
Difficulty breathing or wheezing
Dizziness, fainting, or rapid heartbeat
Widespread hives with breathing difficulty
allergic reaction.follow_up: Seek emergency care immediately for any signs of throat swelling or breathing difficulty; otherwise review with an allergist for long-term management.

possible_medications: Chewable aspirin (300 mg) immediately if heart attack is suspected
Antiplatelets (e.g. clopidogrel, ticagrelor)
Thrombolytics (e.g. alteplase) if primary angioplasty is not immediately available
Beta-blockers (e.g. metoprolol)
Nitrates (e.g. glyceryl trinitrate) for chest pain relief
home_care: Call emergency services immediately — do not attempt to drive yourself
Sit upright and try to remain calm while waiting for help
Chew an aspirin tablet if available and not allergic
After discharge, attend all cardiac rehabilitation sessions
warning_signs: Crushing chest pain or pressure lasting more than a few minutes
Pain radiating to the arm, jaw, neck, or back
Sudden shortness of breath
Cold sweat, nausea, or lightheadedness
heart attack.follow_up: This is a medical emergency — call emergency services immediately rather than waiting for a scheduled appointment.

possible_medications: Sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) spray or tablets for acute episodes
Beta-blockers (e.g. bisoprolol, atenolol) for prevention
Calcium channel blockers (e.g. amlodipine, diltiazem)
Antiplatelets (e.g. aspirin)
Statins (e.g. atorvastatin)
home_care: Stop activity and rest immediately when chest pain begins
Use prescribed GTN spray as directed at the onset of symptoms
Avoid heavy meals, extreme cold, and sudden exertion
Stop smoking and manage blood pressure and cholesterol
warning_signs: Chest pain lasting longer than 15 minutes or not relieved by rest/GTN
Chest pain at rest with no clear trigger
Pain accompanied by sweating, nausea, or breathlessness
New or worsening pattern of chest pain
angina.follow_up: Seek emergency care immediately if pain is not relieved by rest and GTN within a few minutes, as this may indicate a heart attack.

recommended_tests: Arterial or venous blood gas (assess acidosis)
possible_medications: Intravenous fluid resuscitation (0.9% saline) as first-line treatment
Intravenous insulin infusion to lower blood glucose and clear ketones
Potassium replacement once levels and urine output are confirmed adequate
Treatment of any underlying trigger such as infection
home_care: This condition requires emergency hospital treatment and cannot be managed at home
Check blood ketones if blood glucose is very high or unwell with Type 1 diabetes
Never stop insulin during illness — follow sick-day dose-adjustment rules
After recovery, review insulin regimen and sick-day management with the diabetes team
warning_signs: Persistent vomiting and inability to keep fluids down
Deep, rapid breathing
Fruity-smelling breath
Confusion, drowsiness, or loss of consciousness
diabetic ketoacidosis.follow_up: Seek emergency hospital care immediately — diabetic ketoacidosis is a medical emergency requiring inpatient treatment.

recommended_tests: Clinical diagnosis based on symptom pattern and seasonal timing
Skin prick testing for specific pollen allergens
Specific IgE blood testing (RAST) if skin testing is not suitable
possible_medications: Oral antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine)
Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (e.g. fluticasone, mometasone)
Antihistamine eye drops for itchy, watery eyes
Leukotriene receptor antagonists (e.g. montelukast) for persistent symptoms
Allergen immunotherapy for severe or poorly controlled cases
home_care: Monitor local pollen forecasts and stay indoors when counts are high
Keep windows closed during peak pollen season
Shower and change clothes after being outdoors
Use wraparound sunglasses to reduce pollen contact with eyes
Use a saline nasal rinse to clear allergens from the nasal passages
warning_signs: Wheezing or difficulty breathing
Symptoms not responding to standard antihistamines
Swelling of the face, lips, or throat
Significant impact on sleep or daily functioning
seasonal allergies (hay fever).follow_up: See an allergist if symptoms are poorly controlled with standard antihistamines or significantly affect quality of life.

recommended_tests: CT chest for suspected emphysema or complications
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level in early-onset or non-smoking cases
possible_medications: Short-acting bronchodilators (e.g. salbutamol, ipratropium) for symptom relief
Long-acting bronchodilators (e.g. tiotropium, formoterol) for maintenance therapy
Inhaled corticosteroids combined with long-acting bronchodilators for frequent exacerbations
Oral corticosteroids and antibiotics during acute exacerbations
Long-term home oxygen therapy in advanced disease with low blood oxygen
home_care: Stop smoking completely — the single most effective measure to slow progression
Attend pulmonary rehabilitation for exercise training and breathing techniques
Get annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations
Use inhalers correctly — ask a nurse or pharmacist to check technique regularly
Avoid air pollution, dust, and fumes where possible
warning_signs: Increased breathlessness beyond usual baseline
Change in sputum colour, volume, or thickness with fever
Blue lips or fingertips
Confusion, drowsiness, or worsening swelling in the legs
chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (c.follow_up: Regular pulmonology review every 3-12 months depending on severity, with urgent review for any exacerbation.

recommended_tests: Full Blood Count (low haemoglobin, low MCV)
Serum iron and total iron-binding capacity
Stool occult blood test to investigate for gastrointestinal blood loss
Upper and/or lower GI endoscopy if unexplained blood loss is suspected
possible_medications: Oral iron supplements (e.g. ferrous sulfate, ferrous fumarate)
Vitamin C alongside iron to improve absorption
Intravenous iron infusion for severe deficiency or intolerance to oral iron
Blood transfusion in cases of severe symptomatic anaemia
home_care: Eat iron-rich foods such as red meat, leafy greens, and legumes
Take iron supplements with vitamin C-rich foods to improve absorption
Avoid tea, coffee, and calcium supplements close to iron dosing as they reduce absorption
Take iron supplements as prescribed even after symptoms improve, to replenish stores
warning_signs: Chest pain or palpitations
Severe fatigue or fainting
Visible blood in stool or vomit
Rapid worsening of paleness or breathlessness
iron deficiency anemia.follow_up: Recheck Full Blood Count after 4-6 weeks of treatment to confirm response, and investigate any underlying cause of blood loss.
